sections.heading: Amalgamations, mergers and arrangements
sections.title: Bank mergers and asset transfers require regulatory approval
sections.summary: The Minister must consent in writing to mergers, amalgamations, or transfers of assets, liabilities, or assets and liabilities exceeding 25% of a bank's capital. Transfers below 25% require Authority approval if they involve assets aggregating to 10% of on-balance-sheet assets. The Authority must confirm transactions to ensure public interest and bank safety.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit approval for cross-border asset transfers and mergers, directly impacting data transfer DTIAs by mandating regulatory oversight of data flows through merged entities.

requirements.text: Minister must consent in writing to amalgamations involving banks
Minister must consent in writing to transfers exceeding 25% of bank assets
Bank must notify Authority of asset transfers aggregating to less than 10% of on-balance-sheet assets
Authority must confirm transactions do not harm public interest
Transfers must be to another bank or Authority-approved entity
Bank must issue certificate of registration after Authority approval
Transfers must be confirmed at shareholder meetings

sections.source_quote: 54(1) The Minister must consent, in writing and conveyed through the Authority, to- (a) an amalgamation, merger or arrangement referred to in Chapter 5 of the Companies Act and which involves a bank as one of the principal parties to the relevant transaction

sections.heading: Amalgamations, mergers and arrangements (cont.)
Reconstruction within group of companies
sections.title: Bank group reconstructions require regulatory approval
sections.summary: Reconstructions within a bank group that involve annual financial statements under the Companies Act or include banks or their subsidiaries must receive prior written approval from the Authority. This ensures regulatory oversight of structural changes affecting data flows.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates approval for group reconstructions, critical for DTIAs to assess data transfer impacts from structural changes in banking groups.

requirements.text: Reconstructions involving banks or subsidiaries must receive prior written approval from Authority

sections.source_quote: 55 No reconstruction of companies within a group- (a) in respect of which annual financial statements are required to be made out in terms of section 30 of the Companies Act; and (b) of which a bank or a controlling company or subsidiary of a bank is a member, shall be affected without the prior written approval of the Authority.

sections.heading: Alteration of memorandum of incorporation, and change of name
sections.title: Approval required for bank memorandum changes and name alterations
sections.summary: A bank must obtain written approval from the Authority before altering its memorandum of incorporation or changing its name, as required by the Companies Act. The Authority reviews applications submitted with special resolutions and explanations. Banks must provide certified copies of approved resolutions within 21 days of registration. The Authority may reject alterations deemed inconsistent with the Act or undesirable for banks, or names unacceptable under specific criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires formal approval for structural changes to bank governance documents before registration, impacting data transfer assessments by mandating regulatory review of organizational changes that could affect data handling.

requirements.text: Bank must obtain written Authority approval before altering memorandum of incorporation or changing name
Application for Authority approval must be lodged before special resolution is laid at general meeting
Application must include two copies of proposed special resolution and explanation of reasons
Authority may reject alterations inconsistent with Act or undesirable for banks
Authority may reject names unacceptable under specific criteria
Bank must provide certified copy of special resolution within 21 days of registration
Authority issues certificate of registration or name change after payment of fee

sections.source_quote: No alteration... shall have legal force for the purposes of this Act or any other law unless such alteration or change has been approved in writing by the Authority prior to the registration thereof by the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Alteration of memorandum of incorporation in accordance with direction of Authority
sections.title: Authority-directed bank memorandum alterations
sections.summary: The Authority may direct banks to alter their memorandum of incorporation to address anomalies in banking activities. Banks must submit such alterations to shareholders for consideration by the first annual general meeting following the Authority's direction. If a bank refuses, the Authority may treat the alteration as a special resolution submitted to the Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows regulatory oversight of bank structural changes, which could impact data transfer assessments by requiring alignment with regulatory directives before organizational modifications.

requirements.text: Authority may direct bank to alter memorandum of incorporation to remove anomalies in banking activities
Altered memorandum must be submitted to shareholders for consideration by first annual general meeting
Bank must comply with Authority's direction or face treatment as special resolution submitted to Commissioner

sections.source_quote: The Authority may at any time in writing direct a bank to effect such alteration, not contrary to a provision of this Act, to its memorandum of incorporation as the Authority may deem desirable in order to remove anomalies or undesirable divergences in the activities of different banks.

sections.heading: Information regarding directors and officers
sections.title: Bank director and officer records submission
sections.summary: Banks and controlling companies must submit copies of their director and officer records to the Authority within 30 days of registration. This requirement ensures regulatory oversight of key personnel appointments and maintains transparency in banking governance structures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires banks to disclose director and officer information to regulators, which impacts data transfer assessments by establishing baseline personnel data governance.

requirements.text: Bank must submit copy of director and officer records to Authority within 30 days of registration

sections.source_quote: Every bank and every controlling company shall within 30 days of its registration as such, furnish the Authority with a copy of its record of directors and officers.

sections.heading: Returns regarding shareholders
sections.title: Bank shareholder returns submission
sections.summary: Banks and controlling companies must submit shareholder returns to the Authority within 90 days of registration and annually within 30 days of December 31. These returns contain specified information about shareholders, ensuring regulatory transparency in ownership structures.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates regular submission of shareholder data to regulators, which affects data transfer assessments by requiring clear ownership information for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Bank must submit shareholder returns to Authority within 90 days of registration
Bank must submit shareholder returns annually within 30 days of December 31

sections.source_quote: Every bank and every controlling company shall within 90 days of its registration as such, and annually thereafter within 30 days of 31 December of each year, furnish the Authority with a return regarding its shareholders as at the date of the said registration or as on 31 December of that year, as the case may be.

sections.heading: Directors and officers of bank or controlling company
sections.title: Bank director and officer fiduciary duties
sections.summary: Bank directors and officers owe fiduciary duties to the bank, including acting in the bank's best interest, avoiding conflicts of interest, maintaining appropriate knowledge and skills, and exercising reasonable care. The Authority may initiate legal action against directors who knowingly breach these duties, with recovered funds prioritized for depositors.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes fiduciary duties for bank personnel, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring personnel to adhere to strict governance standards when handling data.

requirements.text: Directors and officers must act in bank's best interest
Directors and officers must avoid conflicts of interest
Directors and officers must maintain appropriate knowledge and skills
Directors and officers must exercise reasonable care in their duties
Authority may initiate legal action against directors who knowingly breach duties
Recovered funds from legal action must first cover Authority expenses
Recovered funds must then compensate depositors

sections.source_quote: Each director, chief executive officer and executive officer of a bank owes a fiduciary duty and a duty of care and skill to the bank of which such person is a director, chief executive officer or executive officer.

sections.heading: Directors and officers of bank or controlling company (cont.)
sections.title: Bank director and officer appointment and termination procedures
sections.summary: Banks must notify the Authority of director and officer nominations at least 30 days before appointment. The Authority may object to appointments within 14 days, with disputes resolved through arbitration if the Authority terminates an appointment. The process ensures regulatory oversight of personnel appointments and terminations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires formal appointment procedures with regulatory oversight, impacting data transfer assessments by mandating transparency in personnel changes that affect data handling.

requirements.text: Bank must notify Authority of director/officer nominations at least 30 days before appointment
Authority may object to appointments within 14 days of notification
Authority must notify affected parties before termination of appointment
Affected parties may submit written representations within 14 days
Authority must decide on termination within 14 days of representations
Termination may be referred to arbitration if Authority decides to terminate

sections.source_quote: The Authority may object to the appointment or continued employment of a chief executive officer, director or executive officer of a bank if the Authority reasonably believes that the chief executive officer, director or executive officer concerned is not, or is no longer, a fit and proper person to hold that appointment, or if it is not in the public interest that such chief executive officer, director or executive officer holds or continues to hold such appointment.

sections.heading: Appointment of auditor
sections.title: Auditor appointment approval by Authority
sections.summary: A bank or controlling company must appoint an auditor approved by the Authority. The appointment must be made within 30 days of the Companies Act appointment. The Authority may refuse re-appointments after a prescribed period or withdraw approval if the auditor is convicted, incompetent, under investigation, or fails to disclose conflicts of interest. If approval is denied or withdrawn, the bank must appoint another auditor following the same process.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires auditor appointments to be approved by the Authority, which impacts data transfer DTIAs by ensuring auditors are vetted for independence and competence before handling financial data.

requirements.text: No person may hold office as auditor of a bank unless the Authority approves the appointment
Appoint auditor within 30 days of Companies Act appointment
Authority may refuse re-appointment after prescribed consecutive years
Authority may withdraw approval if auditor is convicted of dishonesty
Authority may withdraw approval if auditor is incompetent
Authority may withdraw approval if auditor is under investigation
Authority may withdraw approval if auditor fails to disclose conflicts of interest
Bank must appoint another auditor if approval is denied or withdrawn

sections.source_quote: no person shall hold office as auditor of a bank or a controlling company unless the appointment of such person as such an auditor has been approved by the Authority

sections.heading: Appointment of auditor by Authority
sections.title: Authority's power to appoint auditor
sections.summary: If a bank fails to appoint an auditor under the Companies Act, the Authority may appoint an auditor directly. This person is deemed to have been appointed by the bank for regulatory purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that banks have auditors approved by the Authority, which is critical for DTIAs involving financial data transfers as it mandates independent oversight.

requirements.text: Authority may appoint auditor if bank fails to appoint under Companies Act
Person appointed by Authority is deemed to have been appointed by bank

sections.source_quote: If a bank for any reason fails to appoint an auditor the Authority may, notwithstanding the provisions of sections 90 and 91 of the Companies Act make the necessary appointment.

sections.heading: Functions of auditor in relation to Authority
sections.title: Auditor's reporting obligations to Authority
sections.summary: Auditors must report to the Authority on irregularities, matters endangering the bank's solvency, or risks to depositor funds. They must also provide written information on matters that could impair financial stability or internal controls. The Authority may share this information with the bank's CEO without legal consequences for the auditor.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires auditors to report to the Authority on financial risks, which impacts DTIAs by ensuring oversight of data handling practices that could affect financial stability.

requirements.text: Auditor must report irregularities to Authority
Auditor must inform Authority of matters endangering bank solvency
Auditor must inform Authority of matters impairing depositor protection
Auditor must inform Authority of matters contrary to sound management
Auditor must inform Authority of matters indicating inadequate internal controls
Auditor may share information with bank's CEO without legal consequences

sections.source_quote: the auditor referred to in section 61 or 62 shall, whenever such auditor furnishes... the Independent Regulatory Board for Auditors with copies of the report... also furnish the Authority with such copies and particulars

sections.heading: Audit committee
sections.title: Audit committee requirements
sections.summary: Banks must form an audit committee with at least three members who are not employees. The committee must assist the board in evaluating internal controls, facilitate communication with auditors, and ensure auditor independence. It must also pre-approve non-audit services and prepare reports on financial statements and internal controls.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates audit committees to oversee financial data handling, which is critical for DTIAs as it ensures robust internal controls and auditor independence for data transfers.

requirements.text: Bank must appoint at least three non-employee committee members
Committee must assist board in evaluating internal controls
Committee must facilitate communication between board and auditor
Committee must ensure auditor independence
Committee must pre-approve non-audit services
Committee must prepare annual report on financial statements

sections.source_quote: the board of directors of a bank and controlling company shall appoint at least three of its members to form and serve on an audit committee

sections.heading: Audit committee (cont.)
sections.title: Board committees: risk, capital and governance oversight
sections.summary: The board must establish and maintain risk and capital management committees with specific functions including monitoring key risks, facilitating communication between board and executives, establishing independent risk management functions, enhancing risk management policies, coordinating global risk monitoring, implementing internal controls, identifying material risks, linking capital to risk levels, setting capital adequacy goals, and ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements. These committees must consist of non-executive directors and operate independently to ensure effective oversight of the bank's risk and capital management processes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific governance structures for risk and capital management that could impact data transfers by requiring robust internal controls and oversight mechanisms, particularly for financial data handling across borders.

requirements.text: Identify and regularly monitor key risks and key performance indicators to maintain decision-making capability and reporting accuracy
Facilitate communication between board and executive officers through reporting structures
Establish independent risk management function with head acting as reference point for risk management
Introduce measures to enhance adequacy and efficiency of risk management policies, procedures, practices and controls
Coordinate monitoring of risk management on a globalised basis
Establish and implement internal controls and reviews to ensure integrity of risk and capital management process
Establish and implement policies to identify, measure and report all material risks
Establish and implement process linking capital to risk levels
Establish and implement capital adequacy goals considering strategic focus and business plan
Perform further functions as may be prescribed
Authority may exempt board from appointing risk and capital management committee if controlling company committee can assume responsibilities

sections.source_quote: The Authority may upon written application exempt the board of directors of a bank from the duty to appoint a risk and capital management committee in respect of a bank if the Authority is satisfied that the risk and capital management committee appointed in respect of the relevant controlling company, in addition to its responsibilities in respect of that controlling company, is able to also adequately assume the responsibilities of a risk and capital management committee in respect of that bank.

sections.heading: Forwarding of certain notices, reports, returns and financial statements to Authority
sections.title: Forwarding financial statements to regulatory authority
sections.summary: Banks must simultaneously forward copies of certain notices, reports, returns and financial statements to the Authority when they are forwarded to shareholders or filed with the Commissioner. This includes notices of meetings, dividend declarations, annual activity reports, changes to registered office, director notices, and financial statements. Banks must also forward minutes of general meetings within 30 days after shareholder meetings to the Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires banks to share financial statements with regulators, which could impact data transfers by mandating specific reporting mechanisms for financial data.

requirements.text: Simultaneously forward copies of notices, reports, returns and financial statements to Authority when forwarded to shareholders
Forward copies of notices of meetings or dividend declarations to Authority
Forward copies of annual activity reports to Authority
Forward copies of changes to registered office to Authority
Forward copies of director notices to Authority
Forward copies of financial statements to Authority
Forward minutes of general meetings within 30 days after shareholder meetings to Authority

sections.source_quote: Whenever a bank or controlling company forwards a notice of a meeting or of the declaration of a dividend or a report on its activities during a financial year or part of such year to its shareholders; files a notice with the Commissioner in terms of section 23(3)(b)(ii) of the Companies Act of any intended change in the situation of its registered office; files in terms of section 70(6) of the Companies Act a notice referred to in that section regarding its directors to the Commissioner; or files in terms of section 33 of the Companies Act, financial statements to the Commissioner, it shall simultaneously forward a copy of such notice, report, return or statements to the Authority.

sections.heading: Disclosure of issued share capital
sections.title: Disclosure of issued share capital in financial statements
sections.summary: If a bank publishes any statement or issues any document mentioning its authorized share capital, it must also disclose its issued share capital in that statement or document.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires banks to disclose share capital information in financial statements, which could impact data transfers by mandating specific disclosures for financial data.

requirements.text: If a bank publishes any statement or issues any document mentioning authorized share capital, it must also mention issued share capital

sections.source_quote: If a bank publishes any statement or issues any document in which the amount of its authorized share capital is mentioned, the amount of its issued share capital shall also be mentioned in such statement or document.

sections.heading: Disclosure of names of certain shareholders
sections.title: Disclosure of large shareholder names in financial statements
sections.summary: If an individual shareholder in a bank holds more than 25% of issued shares with voting rights and the sum of their investments, loans or exposures exceeds the total nominal value of shares held, the bank must disclose the shareholder's name in its financial statements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires disclosure of large shareholders, which could impact data transfers by mandating specific disclosures for financial data.

requirements.text: If individual shareholder holds >25% of issued shares with voting rights and investments/loans/exposures exceed share value, bank must disclose shareholder's name in financial statements

sections.source_quote: If, in the case of an individual shareholder in a bank who holds more than 25 per cent of all the issued shares in that bank to which voting rights are attached, the sum of the amounts of such bank's investments with or loans or advances or other exposures to such individual shareholder exceeds the total nominal value of the said shares so held by that individual shareholder, the bank shall in its financial statements mention the name of such individual shareholder.

sections.title: Repealed section with pending investigations
sections.summary: Section 68 has been repealed by Act 23/2021 effective June 1, 2023, but pending investigations by a commissioner under section 69A must continue as if it had not been repealed.
sections.source_quote: S. 68 amended by s. 16 of Act 9/93, s. 42 of Act 26/94, s. 9 of Act 36/2000, s. 46 of Act 19/2003 and s. 36 of Act 22/2013 and repealed by s. 9(1) of Act 23/2021 w.e.f. 1 June 2023. Despite the amendments to the Banks Act, 1990, contained in subsection 9(1), an investigation by a commissioner in terms of section 69A of the Banks Act, 1990, that is pending and not concluded immediately before the date on which subsection (1) comes into effect must be continued, concluded and reported on by the commissioner in terms of that section as if it had not been repealed.

sections.title: Repealed section with pending investigations
sections.summary: Section 69 has been repealed by Act 23/2021 effective June 1, 2023, but pending investigations by a commissioner under section 69A must continue as if it had not been repealed.
sections.source_quote: S. 69 amended by s. 8 of Act 42/92, s. 17 of Act 9/93, s. 43 of Act 26/94, s. 10 of Act 36/2000, s. 47 of Act 19/2003, s. 47 of Act 169/2003, s. 37 of Act 22/2013 and s. 2 of Act 3/2015 and repealed by s. 9(1) of Act 23/2021 w.e.f. 1 June 2023. Despite the amendments to the Banks Act, 1990, contained in subsection 9(1), an investigation by a commissioner in terms of section 69A of the Banks Act, 1990, that is pending and not concluded immediately before the date on which subsection (1) comes into effect must be continued, concluded and reported on by the commissioner in terms of that section as if it had not been repealed.

sections.heading: Minimum share capital and unimpaired reserve funds
sections.title: Minimum capital and reserve funds for banks
sections.summary: This section mandates that banks maintain minimum capital and reserve funds to ensure financial stability. For banks not trading financial instruments, the minimum capital must be at least R250 million or a prescribed percentage of risk exposures. Banks trading financial instruments must meet similar thresholds. The section specifies how capital and reserve funds are calculated, including adjustments for prescribed amounts and deductions. This section outlines the calculation methods for minimum share capital and unimpaired reserve funds for banks. It specifies that these funds must be taken into account to an amount as may be prescribed and calculated by deducting prescribed amounts from the total. The section also references the substitution of certain provisions by subsequent acts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes minimum capital requirements that impact financial stability, but does not directly address data transfer mechanisms or cross-border data flows relevant to DTIAs.

requirements.text: Banks not trading financial instruments must maintain common equity tier 1 capital, additional tier 1 capital, tier 2 capital, and unimpaired reserve funds totaling at least R250 million or a prescribed percentage of risk exposures.
Banks trading financial instruments must maintain similar capital and reserve funds totaling at least R250 million or a prescribed percentage of risk exposures.
The sum of common equity tier 1 capital and unimpaired reserve funds must be adjusted by prescribed amounts before calculation.
The sum of additional tier 1 capital and unimpaired reserve funds must be adjusted by prescribed amounts before calculation.
The sum of tier 2 capital and unimpaired reserve funds must be adjusted by prescribed amounts before calculation.
Minimum share capital and unimpaired reserve funds shall be taken into account to an amount as may be prescribed
Minimum share capital and unimpaired reserve funds shall be calculated by deducting from the amount thereof such amounts as may be prescribed

sections.source_quote: 70. Minimum share capital and unimpaired reserve funds (1) ... (2) A bank of which the business does not include trading in financial instruments shall manage its affairs in such a way that, subject to the provisions of paragraph (b), the sum of its common equity tier 1 capital, additional tier 1 capital and tier 2 capital and its common equity tier 1 unimpaired reserve funds, additional tier 1 unimpaired reserve funds and tier 2 unimpaired reserve funds in the Republic does not at any time amount to less than the greater of- (i) R250 000 000 or, in the case of such a bank which immediately prior to the date of commencement of this Act was registered as a banking institution or a building society under a law repealed by this Act, R1 000 000; or (ii) an amount which represents a prescribed percentage of the sum of amounts relating to the different categories of assets and other risk exposures and calculated in such a manner as may be prescribed.

sections.heading: Minimum liquid assets
sections.title: Minimum liquid assets requirement for banks
sections.summary: Banks must maintain level one high-quality liquid assets equal to at least a prescribed percentage of their liabilities, but not exceeding 20% of liabilities. The assets must be calculated in a manner and at times specified by regulation. Banks cannot pledge these assets without prior approval from the Authority, which may exempt banks under specific conditions. The assets are valued as prescribed for the purpose of this section.
sections.dtia_summary: Banks must maintain minimum liquid assets to ensure financial stability; this affects cross-border data transfers by requiring sufficient liquidity reserves for international operations.

requirements.text: Banks must hold level one high-quality liquid assets equal to at least a prescribed percentage of their liabilities, but not exceeding 20% of liabilities
Level one high-quality liquid assets and liabilities must be calculated in a manner and at times specified by regulation
Banks cannot pledge level one high-quality liquid assets without prior approval from the Authority
The Authority may exempt banks from the prohibition on pledging assets under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: 72. A bank shall hold in the Republic level one high-quality liquid assets to a value which does not amount to less than the sum of amounts, calculated as prescribed percentages, but which in no instance may exceed 20 per cent, of such different categories of its liabilities as may be specified by regulation with reference to the time when such liabilities fall due or with reference to any other aspect pertaining to such liabilities.

sections.heading: Concentration risk
sections.title: Concentration risk limits for bank investments
sections.summary: Banks cannot make investments or loans exceeding 10% of their capital and reserves without board approval or Authority permission. If investments exceed 800% of capital and reserves, additional capital requirements apply. Banks must report to the Authority when exposures exceed 25% of capital and reserves with specific persons or sectors. The Authority may exempt exposures under conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Concentration risk limits restrict cross-border data transfers by requiring banks to manage exposure to specific entities, ensuring financial stability in international transactions.

requirements.text: Banks cannot make investments or loans exceeding 10% of capital and reserves without board approval or Authority permission
If investments exceed 800% of capital and reserves, additional capital requirements apply
Banks must report to the Authority when exposures exceed 25% of capital and reserves with specific persons
Banks must report to the Authority when exposures exceed 25% of capital and reserves with specific sectors or geographical areas
The Authority may exempt exposures from concentration risk limits under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: 73. A bank, controlling company, branch or branch of a bank- (a) shall not make investments with or grant loans or advances or other credit to any person, to an aggregate amount exceeding 10 per cent of such amount of its capital and reserves as may be prescribed, without first having obtained the permission of its board of directors, or of a committee appointed for such purpose (for the composition of which the prior written approval of the Authority has to be obtained), to make such investments or to grant such loans, advances or other credit; and

sections.heading: Failure or inability to comply with prudential requirements
sections.title: Failure to comply with prudential requirements
sections.summary: Banks must report to the Authority immediately if they fail or are unable to comply with prudential requirements. The Authority may condone failures and allow a specified period to comply. Fines up to 1/10th of 1% of the shortfall per day for sections 70 or 70A, and up to 3% of the shortfall for section 72, may be imposed. Banks must pay fines within the specified period or face civil action.
sections.dtia_summary: Failure to maintain liquidity or capital requirements triggers penalties that impact data transfer stability and regulatory compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Banks must report to the Authority immediately if they fail or are unable to comply with prudential requirements
The Authority may condone failures and allow a specified period to comply
Fines up to 1/10th of 1% of the shortfall per day may be imposed for sections 70 or 70A
Fines up to 3% of the shortfall may be imposed for section 72
Banks must pay fines within the specified period or face civil action

sections.source_quote: 74. If- (a) a bank fails to comply with a provision of section 70 or 72, or is unable to comply with any such provision; or (b) a controlling company fails to comply with a provision of section 70A, or is unable to comply with any such provision, the bank or controlling company shall forthwith in writing report its failure or inability to the Authority, stating the reasons for such failure or inability.

sections.heading: Returns
sections.title: Bank returns and reporting requirements
sections.summary: Banks must submit returns to the Authority to verify compliance with liquidity and capital requirements, asset management, and risk exposures. Returns must follow financial reporting standards and include information on international operations. Banks must report to the Authority when exposures exceed specific thresholds with specific persons or sectors.
sections.dtia_summary: Bank returns ensure transparency in financial operations, which is critical for data transfers across borders to maintain regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Banks must submit returns to the Authority to verify compliance with liquidity and capital requirements
Returns must follow financial reporting standards issued in terms of the Companies Act
Banks must report to the Authority when exposures exceed specific thresholds with specific persons or sectors

sections.source_quote: 75. A bank shall, in order to enable the Authority to determine- (a) whether the bank is complying with the provisions of- (i) sections 70 and 72; or (ii) section 10A of the South African Reserve Bank Act, 1989; and (b) the nature and amounts of the bank's assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities, furnish the Authority, subject to the provisions of subsection (3A), with returns.

sections.heading: Restriction on investments in immovable property and shares, and on loans and advances to certain subsidiaries
sections.title: Restrictions on investments in immovable property and shares
sections.summary: Banks must limit investments in immovable property and shares to a prescribed amount, excluding preference shares. Banks may exceed this limit with Minister approval under specific conditions. The limit is calculated based on book value of investments and loans to subsidiaries.
sections.dtia_summary: Restrictions on investments in immovable property and shares affect data storage and transfer by requiring banks to maintain specific liquidity reserves for international operations.

requirements.text: Banks must limit investments in immovable property and shares to a prescribed amount
Banks may exceed the limit with Minister approval under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: 76. Subject to the provisions of subsection (2), a bank which invests money in immovable property or in shares of any company, or which lends or advances money to any of its subsidiaries of which the main object is the acquisition and holding or development of immovable property, shall manage its transactions in such investments, loans or advances in such a way that the sum of the amounts- (a) invested by it in immovable property, taken at the book value thereof; (b) invested by it in shares of any company (excluding preference shares which are not convertible into ordinary shares), taken at the price at which they were acquired; and (c) owing to it by any such subsidiary in respect of a loan or an advance granted by it, does not at any time exceed a prescribed amount.

sections.heading: Restriction on investments with, and loans and advances to, certain associates
sections.title: Restrictions on investments with certain associates
sections.summary: Banks must limit investments in debentures or preference shares of associates to 10% of a prescribed amount. The limit is calculated by deducting the bank's issued primary share capital and primary unimpaired reserve funds from the total investments. 'Associate' is defined as per section 37(7).
sections.dtia_summary: Restrictions on investments with associates ensure risk management in cross-border data transfers by limiting exposure to specific entities.

requirements.text: Banks must limit investments in debentures or preference shares of associates to 10% of a prescribed amount
The limit is calculated by deducting the bank's issued primary share capital and primary unimpaired reserve funds from the total investments

sections.source_quote: 77. A bank which invests money in debentures or preference shares of any of its associates (excluding any such associate which is a subsidiary referred to in section 76(1), a bank or a mutual building society), or which lends or advances money to any such associate, or which provides guarantees in respect of liabilities of such associates, shall manage its transactions in such investments, loans, advances or guarantees in such a way that the sum of the amounts- (a) invested by it in debentures or preference shares of such associates (excluding debentures or preference shares which are convertible into ordinary shares), taken at the price at which they were acquired; (b) owing to it by such associates in respect of loans or advances granted by it; and (c) of such guarantees, does not at any time exceed ten per cent of the amount prescribed.

sections.heading: Undesirable practices
sections.title: Prohibition on undesirable banking practices
sections.summary: Banks are prohibited from holding shares in subsidiaries, lending against their own shares, granting unsecured loans for share sales, holding assets in third names without authorization, showing certain financial losses in statements, opening branches or paying dividends before provisions for losses, acting as agents for money lending without specific conditions, inflating asset values, and concluding fictitious repurchase agreements. These restrictions apply to all banks and require prior approval from the Authority for dividends.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on banking activities that could affect data transfer by prohibiting certain financial practices that might involve third-party data handling or asset transfers without regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Banks shall not hold shares in any company of which such bank is a subsidiary
Banks shall not lend money to any person against security of its own shares or of shares of that bank's controlling company
Banks shall not grant unsecured loans or loans against security which the Authority deems inadequate for share sales
Banks shall hold all assets in their own name, excluding assets hypothecated for liabilities or approved by the Authority
Banks shall not show financial losses or bad debts in financial statements
Banks shall not open branches or pay dividends before provisions for losses
Banks shall not act as agents for money lending without written contracts specifying lender responsibilities
Banks shall not record assets at inflated values due to losses
Banks shall not conclude repurchase agreements for fictitious assets
Banks shall not conclude repurchase agreements without written documentation and accounting records
Banks shall not pay dividends without prior written approval from the Authority

sections.source_quote: 78. Undesirable practices (1) A bank- (a) shall not hold shares in any company of which such bank is a subsidiary; (b) shall not lend money to any person against security of its own shares or of shares of that bank's controlling company; [Para. (b) substituted by s. 56 of Act 19/2003]

sections.heading: Shares, debentures, negotiable certificates of deposit, share warrants and promissory notes or similar instruments
sections.title: Restrictions on bank share issuance and instruments
sections.summary: Banks are prohibited from issuing no-par-value shares, converting shares to no-par-value shares, issuing preference shares without Authority approval, converting shares to debt instruments without Authority approval, and issuing negotiable certificates of deposit without conditions. The Authority sets limits on debt instruments and negotiable certificates of deposit relative to bank liabilities. Voting rights must be equal for all ordinary shares regardless of nominal value.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts bank share issuance and instruments that could impact data transfer by limiting the types of financial instruments banks can issue, affecting data handling and storage practices.

requirements.text: Banks shall not issue shares of no par value or convert shares into shares of no par value
Banks shall not issue preference shares, hybrid debt instruments, or convert shares into such instruments without Authority approval
Banks shall not issue negotiable certificates of deposit without conditions
The aggregate value of debt instruments and negotiable certificates of deposit shall not exceed a prescribed percentage of bank liabilities
All ordinary shares must have equal voting rights regardless of nominal value

sections.source_quote: 79. Shares, debentures, negotiable certificates of deposit, share warrants and promissory notes or similar instruments (1) A bank shall not- (a) sections 35(2) and 36 of the Companies Act notwithstanding, issue shares of no par value or convert any of its shares into shares of no par value; [Para. (a) substituted by s. 41 of Act 22/2013]

sections.heading: Limitation on certain activities of banks
sections.title: Restrictions on bank ownership of insurers
sections.summary: Banks and their associates cannot hold more than 49% of shares in long-term or short-term insurers without Authority approval. If the ratio is exceeded at the start of the Act, banks may retain shares but cannot acquire further shares until the ratio is restored.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts bank ownership of insurers, which could affect data transfer if banks hold data through insurance subsidiaries.

requirements.text: Banks and their associates shall not hold more than 49% of shares in long-term or short-term insurers without Authority approval
Banks may retain shares if the ratio is exceeded at the commencement of the Act but cannot acquire further shares until the ratio is restored

sections.source_quote: 80. Limitation on certain activities of banks (3) No bank and no associate of a bank shall, without the prior written approval of the Authority, either jointly or individually acquire or hold shares in any registered long-term insurer as defined in section 1 of the Long-term Insurance Act, 1998 (Act of 1998), or in any short-term insurer as defined in section 1 of the Short-term Insurance Act, 1998 (), to the extent to which the nominal value of those shares exceeds 49 per cent of the nominal value of all the issued shares of such insurer.

sections.heading: Order prohibiting anticipated or actual contraventions of certain provisions of Act
sections.title: Prohibition on unregistered persons conducting banking activities
sections.summary: The Authority can prohibit unregistered persons from conducting banking activities that contravene specific sections of the Act, including those involving money laundering or unauthorized banking practices. This includes preventing anticipated or ongoing contraventions of banking regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses potential data transfer risks by prohibiting unregistered persons from engaging in banking activities that could involve data handling without regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: The Authority may prohibit unregistered persons from conducting banking activities that contravene sections 11(1) or 18A(6) of the Act

sections.source_quote: 81. Order prohibiting anticipated or actual contraventions of certain provisions of Act (1) If the Authority has reason to suspect that any person who is not registered as a bank in terms of this Act nor authorized in terms of the provisions of section 18A(1) to carry on the business of a bank- (a) is likely to conduct the business of a bank in contravention of the provisions of section 11(1) or 18A(6); or (b) has so contravened the provisions of section 11(1) or 18A(6) or has contravened the provisions of section 22(4) or (5), or that such a contravention is likely to be continued or repeated, the Authority may apply to a division of the High Court having jurisdiction for an order- (i) prohibiting the anticipated contravention referred to in paragraph (a); (ii) prohibiting the continuation or repetition of a contravention referred to in paragraph (b); or (iii) prohibiting the person concerned from disposing of or otherwise dealing with any of the assets of that person while the contravention suspected of having been committed or of being continued is investigated.

sections.heading: Authority's power to exact information from unregistered persons
sections.title: Information collection from unregistered persons
sections.summary: The Authority can require unregistered persons conducting banking activities to submit financial documents or information related to their affairs. This includes specifying the information to be provided and the timeframe for submission.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures unregistered persons engaging in banking activities provide necessary information, which could impact data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: The Authority may direct unregistered persons to submit documents or information related to their affairs

sections.source_quote: 82. Authority's power to exact information from unregistered persons (1) If the Authority has reason to suspect that any person who is neither registered as a bank nor authorized in terms of the provisions of section 18A(1) to carry on the business of a bank is carrying on the business of a bank, the Authority may by notice in writing direct that person to submit to the Authority such document or to furnish the Authority with such information, relating to the affairs of that person, as the Authority may specify in the notice and as may be available to that person.

sections.heading: Repayment of money unlawfully obtained
sections.title: Repayment of unlawfully obtained money
sections.summary: The Authority can direct persons who obtained money unlawfully through unauthorized banking activities to repay the money, including interest, within a specified period. Failure to comply results in criminal liability and potential insolvency proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses repayment of unlawfully obtained funds, which could impact data transfer if financial data is involved in unauthorized activities.

requirements.text: The Authority may direct persons to repay money obtained unlawfully through unauthorized banking activities

sections.source_quote: 83. Repayment of money unlawfully obtained (1) If as a result of an inspection conducted under section 12 of the South African Reserve Bank Act, 1989 (), the Authority is satisfied that any person has obtained money by carrying on the business of a bank without being registered as a bank or without being authorized, in terms of the provisions of section 18A(1), to carry on the business of a bank, the Authority may in writing direct that person to repay, subject to the provisions of section 84 and in accordance with such requirements and within such period as may be specified in the direction, all money so obtained by that person in so far as such money has not yet been repaid, including any interest or any other amounts owing by that person in respect of such money.

sections.heading: Management and control of repayment of money unlawfully obtained
sections.title: Management of repayment administrator for unlawful money recovery
sections.summary: This section mandates the appointment of a repayment administrator by the Authority to manage and control the repayment of money unlawfully obtained. The administrator must report the person's solvency status, recover assets, stay legal proceedings, and facilitate insolvency processes if insolvent. The Authority may appoint a liquidator or trustee and has specific powers to handle confidential reports and enforce compliance with repayment directives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a mechanism for managing cross-border repayment of unlawfully obtained funds, requiring a repayment administrator to oversee asset recovery and insolvency procedures. It impacts DTIAs by defining specific administrative controls for financial recovery operations.

requirements.text: Authority appoints repayment administrator simultaneously with issuing direction under section 83(1) or as soon as practicable
Repayment administrator reports solvency status to Authority within practicable time
Repayment administrator recovers and takes possession of all assets of person subject to direction
All legal proceedings against person subject to direction must be stayed without court leave and documentation served on Authority
Repayment administrator may apply to court for winding-up if person is insolvent
Authority may oppose applications for winding-up by other persons
Repayment administrator must conduct investigations to establish true amount of unlawfully obtained money
Repayment administrator must identify all persons from whom money was unlawfully obtained
Repayment administrator must locate assets converted from unlawfully obtained money
Repayment administrator must take steps to expedite repayment of unlawfully obtained money
Repayment administrator must report suspected criminal offenses to relevant authorities
Repayment administrator may be paid by Authority for services rendered
Repayment administrator holds office until direction is fully complied with
Authority may withdraw repayment administrator appointment on good cause

sections.source_quote: 84. Management and control of repayment of money unlawfully obtained (1) Simultaneously with the issuing of a direction under section 83(1), or as soon thereafter as may be practicable, the Authority shall by a letter of appointment signed by him or her appoint a person (hereinafter in this section referred to as the repayment administrator) to manage and control the repayment of money in compliance with the direction by the person subject thereto

sections.heading: Certification of returns and other documents
sections.title: Certification of bank returns and documents
sections.summary: This section requires that any return or document submitted to the Authority by a bank or controlling company must be certified as correct by both the chief executive officer and chief accounting officer, and endorsed with the certification date. It establishes procedures for verifying information submitted to the Authority and requires specific certifications for compliance with banking regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by requiring certified documentation for bank returns, which affects data accuracy and compliance verification processes in financial transactions.

requirements.text: Any return or document to be furnished to Authority must be certified by chief executive officer and chief accounting officer
Certification must be endorsed by chief executive officer with date of certification
External credit assessment institutions require prior written approval from Authority to be considered in capital calculations
Applications for external credit assessment institutions must be made in prescribed manner with required information

sections.source_quote: 85. Certification of returns and other documents Any return or other document to be furnished to the Authority by a bank or controlling company in terms of a requirement of this Act, shall be certified as correct by the chief executive officer and, in the case of such a return, also by the chief accounting officer of the bank or controlling company and be endorsed by such chief executive officer with the date on which it is so certified.

sections.heading: Inspection, copies and keeping of documents
sections.title: Public access to banking documents
sections.summary: This section allows the public to inspect, obtain certificates, or copies of specific banking documents held by the Authority, subject to a prescribed fee. It specifies which documents are accessible, including registration certificates, articles of association, and returns, and requires the Authority to retain these documents for at least 10 years (five years for lapsed registrations).
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by defining document retention periods and public access rights for banking records, which impacts data storage and disclosure requirements.

requirements.text: Public may inspect documents held by Authority upon payment of prescribed fee
Public may obtain certificate from Authority regarding document contents
Public may obtain copy or extract of any document held by Authority
Documents include registration certificates, articles of association, and returns
Authority must retain specified documents for at least 10 years
Authority may exempt from fee if public interest is served

sections.source_quote: 86. Inspection, copies and keeping of documents (1) Any person may upon payment of the prescribed fee- (a) inspect any document specified in subsection (2) and kept by the Authority in terms of this Act; (b) obtain a certificate from the Authority as to the contents or any part of the contents of any such document; or (c) obtain a copy of or extract from any such document.

sections.heading: Minors and married women as depositors
sections.title: Minor and married woman depositor rights
sections.summary: This section grants minors over 16 years and married women the right to be depositors with banks without requiring guardian or husband consent. They may execute necessary documents, give acquittances, and deal with deposits as they see fit, while being subject to all depositor privileges and obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining special depositor categories with independent rights, affecting data collection and consent requirements for minors and married women.

requirements.text: Minors over 16 years and married women may be bank depositors without guardian or husband consent
Minors and married women may execute documents, give acquittances, and deal with deposits independently
Husband of married depositor cannot demand deposit details without written consent

sections.source_quote: 87. Minors and married women as depositors (1) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in any law or the common law, and unless otherwise provided in the memorandum of incorporation of a bank, a minor over the age of 16 years or a married woman, whether or not under marital power, may be a depositor with a bank and may without the consent or assistance of his guardian or her husband, as the case may be, execute all necessary documents, give all necessary acquittances and cede, pledge, borrow against, and generally deal with, his or her deposit as he or she thinks fit, and shall enjoy all the privileges and be liable to all the obligations and conditions applicable to depositors.

sections.heading: Limitation of liability
sections.title: Limitation of liability for banking officials
sections.summary: This section limits liability for the South African Reserve Bank, its board members, and officers for losses or damages caused by their bona fide performance of functions under the Act. It explicitly excludes liability for actions taken in good faith to comply with the Act's requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to DTIAs as it addresses liability for banking officials rather than data transfer mechanisms or safeguards.

requirements.text: No liability attaches to South African Reserve Bank or its board members for losses caused by bona fide performance of functions under Act

sections.source_quote: 88. Limitation of liability No liability shall attach to the South African Reserve Bank or, either in his or her official or personal capacity, to any member of the board of directors of the said Bank, the Authority or any other officer or employee of the said Bank, including an inspector duly appointed in terms of section 11 or 12 of the South African Reserve Bank Act,  1989 (), or to a repayment administrator duly appointed in terms of section 84 of this Act, for any loss sustained by or damage caused to any person as a result of anything done or omitted by such member, the Authority or such other officer or employee in the bona fide performance of any function or duty under this Act.

sections.heading: Furnishing of information by Authority
sections.title: Authority information sharing provisions
sections.summary: This section permits the Authority to share information with persons performing functions under law or with foreign authorities for similar purposes, provided the recipient maintains confidentiality. It also requires the Authority to inform the Minister and Governor of the South African Reserve Bank of significant risks to the banking sector.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by defining information sharing protocols with foreign authorities, which affects cross-border data transfer compliance and confidentiality requirements.

requirements.text: Authority may share information with persons performing functions under law if satisfied it's essential for proper performance
Authority may share information with foreign authorities for similar functions if recipient maintains confidentiality
Authority must inform Minister and Governor of significant risks to banking sector

sections.source_quote: 89. Furnishing of information by Authority (1) Notwithstanding the provisions of section 33(1) of the South African Reserve Bank Act, 1989 (), the Authority may furnish information acquired by him or her as contemplated in that section- (a) to any person charged with the performance of a function under any law, provided the Authority is satisfied that possession of such information by that person is essential for the proper performance of such function by that person; or (b) to an authority in a country other than the Republic for the purpose of enabling such authority to perform functions, corresponding to those of the Authority under this Act, in respect of a bank carrying on business in such other country: Provided that the Authority is satisfied that the recipient of the information so provided is willing and able to keep the information confidential within the confines of the laws applicable to the recipient.

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for bank board conduct and financial reporting
sections.summary: The Minister may issue regulations for matters required by this Act, including guidelines for board directors' conduct, financial statement preparation, asset valuation, and payment procedures. These regulations must comply with the Companies Act and may prescribe specific requirements for bank operations. A person deemed to have failed to render a return or statement in a prescribed form must include all required particulars. Regulations may impose fines up to R2,000 or six months imprisonment for contraventions.

requirements.text: Minister may make regulations for matters required or permitted by this Act
Regulations must comply with the Companies Act for board conduct guidelines
Financial statements must follow financial reporting standards under Companies Act
Regulations may prescribe payment procedures for this Act
Regulations may prescribe asset valuation methods
A person must include all required particulars in prescribed returns or statements
Regulations may impose fines up to R2,000 or six months imprisonment for contraventions

sections.source_quote: 90. The Minister may make regulations- (a) as to any matter which is required or permitted to be prescribed by regulation under this Act; (b) subject to the provisions of the Companies Act, providing guidelines relating to the conduct of, and prescribing requirements to be complied with by, a member of the board of directors of a bank in the performance of the functions of such a director; [Para. (b) substituted by s. 64 of Act 19/2003]

sections.heading: Offences and penalties
sections.title: Offences and penalties for bank misconduct
sections.summary: This section defines offences and penalties for failing to comply with regulatory directions, providing false information to the Authority, and engaging in improper transactions with banks. It covers penalties for directors and employees who accept benefits or purchase property without authorization, and banks that pay dividends during shortfalls. Offenders face fines up to R10,000 or imprisonment for up to ten years.

requirements.text: Failing to comply with a direction under section 7 constitutes an offence
Furnishing false information to the Authority constitutes an offence
Contravention of specified sections constitutes an offence
Directors or employees who accept benefits without authorization constitute an offence
Directors or employees who purchase property without written consent constitute an offence
Banks paying dividends during shortfalls constitute an offence
Offenders face fines up to R10,000 or imprisonment for up to ten years

sections.source_quote: 91. Any person who- (a) fails to comply with a direction under section 7; (aA) in the completion of any questionnaire... furnishes the Authority with any information which to the knowledge of such person is untrue or misleading in any material respect; or (b) contravenes or fails to comply with a provision of section 7(3), (4) or (5), 34, 35, 38(1), 39, 41, 42(1), 52(1) or (4), 53, 55, 58, 59, 60(5)(a), 60(5)(b), 61(2), 65, 66, 67, 70(2), (2A) or (2B), 70A, 72, 73, 75, 76, 77, 78(1) or (3), 79, 80, 84(1A) or 84(2), shall be guilty of an offence.

sections.heading: Review of Act
sections.title: Review of the Banks Act
sections.summary: The Minister must appoint a standing committee to review this Act periodically. Committee members serve for determined terms and may recommend amendments to the Minister based on changed circumstances or administrative needs. The committee uses the Policy Board for Financial Services and Regulation to make recommendations.

requirements.text: Minister must appoint a standing committee to review this Act periodically
Committee members serve for determined terms and may be reappointed
Committee may recommend amendments to the Minister through the Policy Board for Financial Services and Regulation

sections.source_quote: 92. The Minister shall appoint a standing committee to review this Act from time to time, and such committee shall consist of such incumbents of offices and other members as the Minister may from time to time determine.

sections.heading: Interpretation of certain references in existing laws and in other documents
sections.title: Interpretation of references to banks in existing laws
sections.summary: References to deposit-taking institutions, banking institutions, or building societies in existing laws prior to the 1993 amendment are construed as references to banks. Specific provisions in the Income Tax Act are interpreted to include banks that were previously building societies or banking institutions.

requirements.text: References to deposit-taking institutions, banking institutions, or building societies are construed as references to banks
References in the Income Tax Act to building societies are construed as references to banks that were previously building societies
References in the Income Tax Act to deposit-taking institutions are construed as references to banks that were previously banking institutions

sections.source_quote: 93. A reference in any law in force immediately prior to the commencement of the Deposit-taking Institutions Amendment Act, 1993, or in any other document, to- (a) a deposit-taking institution, discount house, banking institution, banking institution registered under or in terms of the Banks Act, 1965 (), or building society registered in terms of the Building Societies Act, 1986 (), shall, unless inconsistent with the context or otherwise clearly inappropriate, be construed as a reference to a bank;

sections.heading: Amendment of section 3 of Act 61 of 1973, as amended by section 106 of Act 82 of 1986
sections.title: Amendment of Companies Act section 3
sections.summary: Section 3 of the Companies Act is amended to include references to banks or insurance companies where provisions are inconsistent with this Act. This amendment affects how companies are classified under the Companies Act.

requirements.text: Section 3 of the Companies Act is amended to include references to banks or insurance companies

sections.source_quote: 94. Section 3 of the Companies Act is hereby amended by the substitution for paragraph (b) of subsection (1) of the following paragraph: "(b) with reference to any company or external company or society which is subject to the provisions of any law relating to [banks or] insurance companies or societies in so far as those provisions are inconsistent with the provisions of this Act; or"

sections.heading: Short title
sections.title: Short title of the Banks Act
sections.summary: This Act is officially titled the Banks Act, 1990. This section establishes the name of the legislation without imposing operational requirements.

requirements.text: This Act shall be called the Banks Act, 1990

sections.source_quote: 96. This Act shall be called the Banks Act, 1990.
